coredata.dc:title: Modification and performance enhancement of P3HT:PCBM based organic solar cells incorporated with phosphorus doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Kırbıyık Kurukavak, Çisem
Büyükbekar, Alihan
Tok, Mütahire
Yılmaz, Tuğbahan
Kuş, Mahmut
Ersöz, Mustafa
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate a strategy to improve the performance of organic solar cells (OSCs) by phosphorous doped CQDs (P-CQDs). The presence of P-CQDs P3HT:PCBM layer (1 vol% of P-CQDs) enhanced photon harvesting in the UV region and emitting visible light, the PCE was increased from 2.19% to 2.48% and champion device provided a FF of 55.34%. The incorporation of P-CQDs leads to effective photons harvesting ability and collection/transport of charge carriers. The optical absorption and crystallinity of photoactive layer improved with the incorporation of P-CQDs. The larger grain size distribution were obtained with P-CQDs additive helping to better charge extraction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Heteroatom doping
P3HT:PCBM
Additive engineering
Organic solar cell

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank to financial support of EngSurf-Twin project (GA: 952289) European Union's Horizon 2020, Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) (PN: 121F377), and Konya Technical University Scientific Research Council (PN: 221016007).

In commemoration of the 100th anniversary of our Republic, we express our gratitude to Gazi Mustafa Kemal Atat\u00FCrk and his comrades-in-arms for their leadership and sacrifices towards Turkey's independence and modernisation. This work is dedicated to them. The authors wish to convey their sincere appreciation to the unidentified reviewers for their feedback and recommendations aimed at enhancing the quality of the article. The authors would like to thank to financial support of EngSurf-Twin project (GA: 952289) European Union's Horizon 2020, Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) (PN: 121F377), and Konya Technical University Scientific Research Council (PN: 221016007). Not applicable. Not applicable

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The absorption spectrum showing the calculated band-gap and (b) the cyclic voltammetry curve of the P-CQDs.
The 2D AFM images of P3HT:PCBM thin films prepared with different vol% of P-CQDs.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra and (b) normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM thin films with different vol% of P-CQDs.
The XRD analysis of P3HT:PCBM thin films with different vol% of P-CQDs, P3HT and PCBM (1 0 0) peaks.
(a) The device architecture and (b) carrier transport mechanism.
The average roughness (Ra), grain size distribution, crystal size, and Eg values P3HT:PCBM films prepared with different vol.% of P-CQDs.
Photovoltaic performance, the series resistance (Rs) and shunt resistance (Rsh) values of organic solar cells based on different vol.% of P-CQDs extracted from Fig. 5 (a).
Performance comparison of P3HT:PCBM organic solar with various additives cells reported in recent studies.

head.ce:title: Modification and performance enhancement of P3HT:PCBM based organic solar cells incorporated with phosphorus doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electric and Energy, Konya Technical University, 42075 Konya, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy Services, Vocational School, Yuksek Ihtisas University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate a strategy to improve the performance of organic solar cells (OSCs) by phosphorous doped CQDs (P-CQDs). The presence of P-CQDs P3HT:PCBM layer (1 vol% of P-CQDs) enhanced photon harvesting in the UV region and emitting visible light, the PCE was increased from 2.19% to 2.48% and champion device provided a FF of 55.34%. The incorporation of P-CQDs leads to effective photons harvesting ability and collection/transport of charge carriers. The optical absorption and crystallinity of photoactive layer improved with the incorporation of P-CQDs. The larger grain size distribution were obtained with P-CQDs additive helping to better charge extraction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic solar cells (OSCs) have emerged as one of promising alternative to replace traditional silicon-based solar cells since they offer numerous advantages, including lightweight, flexibility, and long-term device stability [1]. In addition, the cost-effective and easy fabrication processes by solution-based methods make OSCs feasible for mass production [2]. Bulk-heterojunction (BHJ) architectures of OSCs are widely investigated, in which BHJ is a bicontinuous interpenetrating network consisting of electron donor and acceptor materials sandwiched between two electrodes [3]. To form a BHJ blend, poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT) and (6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PCBM) are often used as electron donor and acceptor combination, respectively [4]. However, the P3HT:PCBM based OSCs have been frequently reported to present a lower power conversion efficiency (PCE) than that of inorganic counterparts (B. [5]. The poor crystallinity of P3HT and the low electrical conductivity of photoactive blend are main reasons of low PCE [6,7]. These two issues directly affect the charge carrier generation, extraction from photoactive blend, recombination, and thus charge transport properties in OSCs [8]. The incorporation of third component into the photoactive blend has been gained a significant attention by researchers since it is an easy and effective approach to improve the photovoltaic characteristics [9].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanoparticles, can be a good third component to provide better device performance. CQDs present excellent properties such as nontoxicity, tunable optical/electrical properties, biocompatibility, and surface functionalization [10]. There are several studies on the incorporation of CQDs in OSCs as an additive into photoactive blend. For example, Zhang et al. obtained a 43.2 % enhancement in PCE of OSCs based on P3HT:indene–C60 bisadduct layer prepared with the addition of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) [11]. More recently in 2023, a notable photovoltaic performance enhancement by the combined effect of co-introducing isopropanol (IPA) and CQDs into OSCs with the configuration of FTO/ZnO/P3HT:PCBM:CQDs:IPA/Ag was reported by Wajidh et al. [12]. It is worth to say that the presence of CQDs in photoactive layer could provide better light absorption and charge separation/transport [13]. Additionally, the suppression of leakage current and electron accumulation can be enabled, which helps to reduce the interface recombination [14]. As well-known, the properties of CQDs can be tailored by simple methods, including heteroatom doping or surface passivation [15]. In particular, non-metal heteroatom doping has been reported to be an effective method for improved photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and other properties of CQDs due to the formation of new excited energy potentials by adjusting surface chemistry of carbon frameworks [16]. With this investigation, instead of the non-doped CQDs using, we studied the influence of incorporating phosphorous doped carbon quantum dots (P-CQDs) on the efficiency of OSCs.

The recent developments in nanomaterials fields showed that the heteroatom functionalities in CQDs additive can alter the energy band gap [17]. Our previous study showed how boron doped CQDs helps the enhancing charge separation and transport in OSCs [18]. The reason for the choice of P-CQDs: (i) electron-rich phosphorus can act as a core to form better phase separation in photoactive blend. (ii) The poor carrier mobility and charge recombination problem because of the thickness of OSCs can be eliminated by the incorporation of CQDs as electron acceptor materials [19]. (iii) The presence of phosphorous on CQDs surface can decrease the interplanar distance like graphitic carbon framework. (iv) Finally, P-CQDs additive with good crystalline structure can act as bridges, promoting molecular aggregation and improved crystallinity of photoactive layer. Therefore, the determination the specific role of phosphorous doping in CQDs, which might cause significant differences in photoactive layer, is worth to investigate. In this study, the use of various concentrations of P-CQDs as additive in P3HT:PCBM photoactive blend was demonstrated, which has not been investigated so far. The optimized ratio of P-CQDs P3HT:PCBM layer (1 vol% of P-CQDs) resulted almost 14 % improvement in PCE in comparison to that of device without P-CQDs. Additionally, the morphological, structural, and optical properties of P3HT:PCBM thin films prepared with the addition of P-CQDs were analysed in detailed. Especially, the improved phase separation of active layer with the higher aggregation of P3HT promotes the device stability and better exciton dissociation and charge-transfer [20]. The increased device performance was mainly due to the effective collection/transport of charge carriers and improvement in crystallinity of P3HT. With the P-CQDs as a third component that we utilized in this research, one can easily control and tune the morphological/structural properties and band gap energy of photoactive layer and improve the efficiency of OSCs.

P-CQDs used were synthesized by solvothermal procedure from three precursors according to a method reported in our previous study [21] by dissolving of 1.5 g of citric acid (Sigma-Aldrich, >99 %), 1.5 g of urea (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥ 99.5 %), 5 mL of concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4, Sigma-Aldrich) solution with 100 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Sigma-Aldrich, anhydrous, 99.8 %) in a Teflon-coated autoclave for 24 h at 180 °C, resulting to a dark-brown solution. After purification and centrifugation, the P-CQDs was filtered with a 0.22-μm PTFE filter. Finally, 10 mg of P-CQDs participate was dispersed in 1 mL of in chloroform/chlorobenzene solvent mixture in a volume ratio of 1:1 and sonically stirred for 30 min. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum and the PL spectra of P-CQDs were obtained using a Biochrom Libra S22 and a Perkin Elmer LS-55 photoluminescent spectrometers, respectively. The morphology, size, and shape of the P-CQDs were analysed using a JEOL JEM 2100 FHRTEM transmission electron microscope (TEM).

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) method was conducted using a standard three-electrode cell system using a Metrohm 400, μStart potentiostat/galvanostat apparatus fitted with a C3 cell stand. 0.071 cm2 geometric area BAS Model MF-2012 GC electrodes were used as working electrodes. Ag/Ag+ (in 10 mM AgNO3) in a non-aqueous solution was utilized as the reference electrode (to prepare an Ag/Ag+ reference electrode, 10 mM AgNO3 was dissolved in acetonitrile (CH3CN) with 100 mM tetrabutylammoniumtetrafluoroborate (TBATFB). Just before the electrochemical treatment, all solutions utilized were treated for five minutes with argon (99.999 %). The electrodes were cleaned using a procedure to get rid of any oxidation products and create a fresh and clean electrode surface before being employed in electrochemical testing. The GC electrode's surface was polished using a polishing cloth in circular motions for 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm alumina-water slurry, as per this protocol. The electrodes were then cleaned and sonicated in pure water for 10 min. After that, the electrodes were cleaned with CH3CN and sonicated for ten minutes using a 1:1 (v/v) mixture of CH3CN and isopropyl alcohol to get rid of any contaminants that water was unable to remove. The surface of the GC electrode was modified by dropping P-CQD prepared at a concentration of 10 mg/mL onto the cleaned surfaces and drying it. In non-aques media of tetra-n-butylammoniumtetrafluoroborate (Bu4NBF4) at 100 mM concentration, it scans at 100 mV/s in the potential range from −1.5 V to + 1.5 V, Pt wire was replaced as the counter electrode, Ag /Ag+ as the reference electrode and working electrode. Measurements were taken in 2 cycles using the cyclic voltammetry technique with the GC electrode. Reduction and oxidation peak regions were determined by taking 2-cycle voltammograms in the solution.

The OSCs with structure of glass/indium tin oxide (ITO)/PEDOT:PSS (60 nm)/P3HT:PCBM (200 nm)/ LiF (2 nm)/Al (100 nm) were fabricated. Firstly, glass substrates were cleaned by diluted Hellmanex III (Sigma Aldrich, 1:50 in water) solution, deionize (DI) water, acetone, isopropanol, and DI water again sequentially in ultrasonic bath, and followed by treating with nitrogen gun. The poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS, Heraeus Group, Clevios™ P VP Al 4083) thin film was spin-coated on top of the cleaned ITO substrate as hole transport layer at 2000 rpm for 60 s, followed by annealing at 120 °C for 30 min in air. The P3HT (Sigma-Aldrich):PCBM (Lumtec) photoactive solutions were prepared in a mass ratios of in chloroform/chlorobenzene solvent mixture in a volume ratio of 1:1 (40 mg/mL) under stirring at 70 °C for 24 h. Then, 1, 3, 5, and 7 vol% of P-CQDs with respect to volume of P3HT:PCBM solution were incorporated. The photoactive blend solution was spin-coated at room temperature of ∼ 25 °C at 2000 rpm for 60 s. After the coating, the substrates were baked at 120 °C for 30 min. At the end of the coating, using a physical vapor deposition technique, 2 nm of LiF and 100 nm of Al were deposited as cathode at 1 × 10−6 mbar with a shadow mask of 0.023 cm2.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the thin films were obtained by same instrument mentioned above. The morphological analysis and crystallization of photoactive layer were illuminated by NT-MDT NTEGRA Solaris Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) and Bruker Advance D8 X-ray diffractometer (XRD), respectively. The current density–voltage (J-V) characteristics were recorded using a Keithley 2400 under light source intensity of 100 mA cm−2, 1.5 AM provided by ATLAS solar simulator.

The representative TEM images and particle size distribution of P-CQDs are given in Fig. S1, which proves that the P-CQDs were successfully synthesized in quantum size ranging from 2 to 4 nm. This makes P-CQDs useful for improved photoactive layers used in light-scattering processes and promoted short-circuit current density (JSC) in solar cells [22].

To investigate optical properties, UV–vis absorption spectrum and the PL spectra of P-CQDs were recorded (Fig. S2(a) and S2 (b)). P-CQDs showed one typical absorption peak at around 300 nm, which were related to the n–π * transitions of the C = O and C = P bonds. The emission spectra of the P-CQDs shows broad PL peaks at around 450 nm at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Both results show that the P-CQDs is a promising additive in photoactive layer due to absorption in visible region leading to better photon absorption. Additionally, the bandgap energy (Eg) as a function of photon energy hν vs. (αhν)2 was calculated from UV–vis absorption data. The P-CQDs resulted an Eg of 1.98 eV (seen in Fig. 1 (a)), which leads P-CQDs to dissociate and transfer the charge carriers by easily contributing and embedding into active layer [23]. For further investigation of energy level alignment of thin films, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy levels were calculated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques (seen in Fig. 1 (b)). P-CQD exhibited two reversible oxidation peaks (EI 1/2 = 1.013 V, EII 1/2 = 1.27 V vs. Ag/Ag+) when positive region scanning was performed. In the negative region scan (EIII 1/2 = -0.46 V EIV 1/2 = -1.37 V vs. Ag/Ag+), two reversible oxidation peaks were observed. Energy values were calculated using the ferrocene value of −4.4 eV relative to vacuum level, defined as zero. HOMO and LUMO values are calculated according to relevant equations with reference to −4.4 eV for ferrocene [24,25]. The energy band gap was calculated to be 1.717 eV from the cyclic voltammogram. The HOMO and LUMO values were determined from the oxidation and reduction peaks in the cyclic voltammogram, resulting in 5.413 and 3.696 eV, respectively. It was observed that the Eg values obtained from the measurements made by cyclic voltammetry and UV–vis spectroscopy to determine the energy levels of P-CQD are compatible with each other.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) present the UV–Vis absorption spectra and normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM thin films with and without P-CQDs addition, respectively. All samples showed similar absorbance in the range of 300–400 nm related to absorption of PCBM and in the range of 400–650 nm related to π–π* electronic transition of P3HT, which matches the band gap values reported in literature (F.-C. [28]. The sample prepared with the addition of 1 vol% of P-CQDs exhibited slightly higher absorption than that of bare P3HT:PCBM sample, but the increasing concentration of P-CQDs addition reduced the set of curves. This could be correlated with the fact that phosphorus have better absorption compared to some polymers [29]. Even though it can absorb better the solar spectrum, the increasing addition of P-CQDs solution volume can decrease the thin film density, thereby optical absorption. At the same time, it should be noted that there is no substantive difference with the variation in vol.% of P-CQDs addition. In Fig. 3 (b), it was revealed that the absorption in the range of 300 nm–4000 nm is higher than that of the bare film indicating the presence of more PCBM domains. This presented a slight increase in the optical density of the photoactive film and related to the well-ordered polymer film. One can clearly see that the addition of P-CQDs broaden the wavelength range, which could be attributed to the increment the effect of reflecting and scattering [30]. Furthermore, to determine the Eg values of P3HT:PCBM thin films, Tauc plots were used again and the direct bandgaps were found [31,32]. The results (Table 1 and Fig. S3) showed that the Eg values increased with the increasing vol.% concentration of P-CQDs, then decreased. Compared to bare P3HT:PCBM thin film, the incorporation of P from P-CQDs widened the energy gap as a result of increased carrier concentrations, which is known as Burstein–Moss effect [33,34]. A wider bandgap value results in increase in JSC in OSCs due to the absorption of photons with higher energy levels [35]. On the other hand, the Eg value reduced for the P3HT:PCBM surface prepared with the addition of 7 vol% of P-CQDs, since the more defect states formed owing to high aggregation [36]. It should be noted that there is a complex relationship between optical properties and additive concentration, which strongly effects the functionality of OSCs. Since the optical band gap depends on film thickness, deposition technique and crystallinity of thin films, it is interesting it is interesting that the Eg also increases with film crystallinity [37,38].

Another important indicator of the effectiveness of exciton dissociation and the charge transfer efficiency of donor–acceptor composite materials is the PL of the bulk heterojunction layer [39]. The PL spectra were represented in Fig. S4. The bare P3HT: PCBM thin film exhibited PL peaks at 632 nm and 733 nm, whereas P3HT:PCBM thin films prepared with the addition of P-CQDs showed peaks at 630 nm and 684 nm. With the addition of P-CQDs, the peak at 733 nm was blue-shifted to 684 nm, which related to band-to-band emission in P3HT since it showed a wider band gap value [40]. These results are in a good matching with the UV–Vis absorption results. With increasing P-CQDs concentration to 5 vol%, the intensity of PL increased, leading improved exciton split, which helps to obtain high-efficiency OSCs [41]. Additionally, the increased PL intensity is an indicator of improved surface morphology. The enhanced diffusion of PCBM into the P3HT provides better the energy and charge carrier transfer due to the increased phase separation areas onto photoactive layer [6,7]. Besides, PL intensity can be increased through the excitation of surface plasmons owing to extended light-molecules interaction [42]. Note that the PL intensity also reduced for higher additive concentration, but the decrease was less dramatic than the increase.

The improvement in the crystallinity of P3HT:PCBM thin films prepared with the addition of P-CQDs was investigated by recording their XRD patterns (given in Fig. 4 ). The characteristic P3HT peaks for the (100) plane was observed at around 5.5°, while the characteristic PCBM peaks for the (100) plane was observed at around 21.5° [43]. It can be seen that both peak intensities of P3HT:PCBM films prepared with the addition of P-CQDs were higher than those of the bare film. Considering these improvements, one can conclude that the polymer chains ordering enhanced by the incorporation of P-CQDs in active layer. Additionally, the calculated crystallite sizes changing from 8.7 nm without P-CQDs addition to 14–16 nm in P-CQDs addition agreed with the AFM results. The highest crystallite size was found to be around 16.2 nm for the P3HT:PCBM surface prepared with the addition of 3 vol% of P-CQDs. As seen in Table 1, the crystallite size was decreased with the increased additive concentration (14.7 nm for 5 % and 15.9 nm for 7 %), which could be related to stacking defects in P3HT: PCBM photoactive layer [44]. It proves that the incorporation of P-CQDs in P3HT:PCBM layer is valuable to the photovoltaic characteristics, since enhanced polymer chain ordering show a significant effect on charge separation and transport [45].

Photovoltaic performance of organic solar cells based on different vol.% of P-CQDs measured by Fig. 5 (a) are listed in Table 2 . The reference device without P-CQDs addition showed the lowest efficiency of 2.189 % with an short-circuit current density (JSC) of 6.80 (mA cm−2), open-circuit voltage (VOC) of 0.598 V, and fill factor (FF) of 53.83 %, while the best performance was obtained by the addition of 1 vol% of P-CQDs with an efficiency of 2.482 %, thanks to improvements in JSC (7.461 mA cm−2), and FF (55.34 %). The results obtained are comparable with the efficiencies reported in literature (Table 3 ) [46,47,48,6,7]. The improved device performance by the incorporation of P-CQDs was mainly due to the better surface contact and modulation of Eg values between layers leading to high charge carrier collection and transfer [43]. On the other hand, the agglomeration formation in photoactive layer by the increased vol% of P-CQDs addition can lead to charge recombination decreasing the FF values [11]. The agglomerated domains could provide a leakage path for the charge carriers. When the addition of P-CQDs exceeds a certain concentration, the stacked layers derive a parasitic path for the current (C. [49]. When the P-CQDs addition concentration was more than 1 vol%, the FF value of OSCs was slightly decreased due to the damaged interpenetrated network of bulk layer. By Pareek et al., similar phenomenon was reported on graphitic carbon nitride QDs (g-C3N4) added in P3HT:PC71BM system [44].

The calculated internal resistance (series resistance (Rs) and shunt resistance (Rsh)) values were also consistent with this slight decrease in FF of devices, since the agglomeration of P-CQDs can hinder the charge transportation, thus increase the Rs and decrease the Rsh values [50,51]. Interestingly, the device prepared by the addition of 1 vol% of P-CQDs exhibited the lowest Rsh of 357.65 Ω cm−2 than those of other devices, even though this OSC showed the lowest Rs of 17.10 Ω cm−2. The decrease in the Rsh may occur because of different imperfections. The most basic ones of these are the presence of pinholes on the bulk layer and the low ohmic contacts across the junction due to the fast diffusion of additive materials on grain boundaries [52]. However, it can be said that the decreased in Rs could compensate for the decrease in Rsh, resulting in an improve in FF. These results indicate that the proper vol% of P-CQDs addition into P3HT:PCBM layer can enhance the crystallinity, band gap matching, and charge transfer mechanism. Fig. 5 (b) shows the energy level diagram of reference device and P-CQDs incorporated devices. The both valance and conduction bands of P-CQDs are in a good matching with the HOMO and LUMO levels of P3HT and PCBM, providing smooth charge transportation. Furthermore, improved Rsh and reduced Rs values showed that better electron extraction and transfer can be carried out for P-CQDs incorporated OSCs.

In conclusion, the P-CQDs were firstly synthesized in quantum size ranging from 2 to 4 nm through hydrothermal method by using low-cost initial materials, which shows high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY, ∼23 %). The energy band gap of CQDs was calculated to be 1.717 eV from the CV. The optical and electrical properties of P-CQDs can lead to dissociate and transfer the charge carriers by easily contributing and embedding them into active layer. The influence of incorporating blue-emitting P-CQDs with different concentration (0, 1, 3, 5, and 7 vol%) into P3HT:PCBM thin films on the efficiency of OSCs with ITO/PEDOT:PSS/P3HT:PCBM:P-CQDs/LiF/Al structure was investigated. The incorporation of optimized ratio of P-CQDs (1 vol% of P-CQDs) improved the optical absorption and crystallinity of photoactive layer, as well as the film morphology, leading to efficient hole carrier transfer through the films. Additionally, the incorporation of a sufficient amount of P-CQDs provided a better surface contact and modulation of Eg values between layers leading to high charge carrier collection and transfer. Blue emitting P-CQDs can absorb the UV region and emit visible light in photoactive layer, which improve the capability of light harvesting by photovoltaic device. On the other hand, the energy level alignment reduced for the P3HT:PCBM surface prepared with the addition of excessive P-CQD concentration, since the more defect states formed owing to high aggregation. The highest efficiency of 2.482 % was obtained at an incorporation of 1 vol% of P-CQDs, thanks to improvements in JSC (7.461 mA cm−2), and FF (55.34 %). According to these benefits, P-CQDs incorporating strategy has potential approaches for achieving better performance of OSCs.

Çisem Kırbıyık Kurukavak: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Alihan Büyükbekar: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Mütahire Tok: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Tuğbahan Yılmaz: Writing – review & editing. Mahmut Kuş: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Mustafa Ersöz: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: In commemoration of the 100th anniversary of our Republic, we express our gratitude to Gazi Mustafa Kemal Atatürk and his comrades-in-arms for their leadership and sacrifices towards Turkey’s independence and modernisation. This work is dedicated to them.
The authors wish to convey their sincere appreciation to the unidentified reviewers for their feedback and recommendations aimed at enhancing the quality of the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors would like to thank to financial support of EngSurf-Twin project(GA:952289) European Union's Horizon 2020, Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) (PN: 121F377), and Konya Technical University Scientific Research Council (PN: 221016007).
Not applicable.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141300.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of surface tension and drying time on inkjet-printed PEDOT: PSS for ITO-free OLED devices
J. Sci.: Adv. Mater. Devices

Appl. Phys. A

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural modification and conductivity enhancement of NiCl2 incorporated P3HT: PC60BM based organic photoactive layer
Opt. Mater.
